Brexit elasticities core results documentation

Prepare the dataset

This chunk loads the main dataset and prepares the analysis sample. First, the Excel file containing the full applicant dataset is imported (it is also pre-filtered). Non-comparable territories and aggregate categories are removed to ensure that only consistent country units remain. Two country groups are then defined: EU member states (eu_real), which represent the treated group, and a synthetic comparison set (eu_synth) used as the control pool. The dataset is restricted to observations belonging to these two groups and to years after 2015. Finally, indicator variables are created to identify whether each observation belongs to the real EU group or the synthetic comparison group, and a categorical grouping variable is constructed for convenience. Basic checks (unique countries, group counts, and data preview) are printed to verify the resulting sample.

library(readxl)
library(dplyr)

data <- read_excel(
  "data_filtered.xlsx", 
  sheet = "full_data"
)

data <- data %>%
  filter(!Domicile_named_country %in% c(
    "St Barthelemy", "St Martin (French part)", "Reunion",
    "Martinique", "Guadeloupe", "Aland Islands",
    "Cyprus (Not otherwise specified)",
    "French West Indies (Not In Use)",
    "Canary Islands", "French Guiana"
  ))

eu_real <- c(
  "Austria", "Belgium", "Bulgaria", "Croatia", "Cyprus (European Union)",
  "Czech Republic", "Denmark", "Estonia", "Finland", "France",
  "Germany", "Greece", "Hungary",  "Italy",
  "Latvia", "Lithuania", "Luxembourg", "Malta", "Netherlands",
  "Poland", "Portugal", "Romania", "Slovakia", "Slovenia",
  "Spain", "Sweden"
)
eu_synth <- c("Australia", "Saudi Arabia", "Russian Federation", "Peru", "Kuwait", "Bahrain","Singapore","Switzerland", "Hong Kong", "United States of America",  "Thailand", "Canada", "Mauritius", "Qatar", "Norway", "Israel", "Malaysia", "Jordan", "Albania", "Serbia", "Ireland", "Jersey", "Macao","United Arab Emirates", "Vietnam [Viet Nam]", "Turkey")


data <- data %>%
  filter(Domicile_named_country %in% c(eu_real, eu_synth))

data_UCAS <- data %>%
  mutate(
    real_EU  = ifelse(Domicile_named_country %in% eu_real, 1, 0),
    synth_EU = ifelse(Domicile_named_country %in% eu_synth, 1, 0),
    EU_group = case_when(
      Domicile_named_country %in% eu_real ~ "Real_EU",
      Domicile_named_country %in% eu_synth ~ "Synthetic_EU",
      TRUE ~ "Other"
    )
  )

print(unique(data$Domicile_named_country))
 [1] "Austria"                  "Belgium"                  "Bulgaria"                 "Denmark"                 
 [5] "Finland"                  "France"                   "Germany"                  "Greece"                  
 [9] "Hungary"                  "Ireland"                  "Italy"                    "Luxembourg"              
[13] "Malta"                    "Netherlands"              "Poland"                   "Portugal"                
[17] "Romania"                  "Spain"                    "Sweden"                   "Estonia"                 
[21] "Latvia"                   "Lithuania"                "Croatia"                  "Slovenia"                
[25] "Czech Republic"           "Slovakia"                 "Cyprus (European Union)"  "Jersey"                  
[29] "Russian Federation"       "Mauritius"                "Norway"                   "Peru"                    
[33] "Qatar"                    "Saudi Arabia"             "Singapore"                "Switzerland"             
[37] "Thailand"                 "United Arab Emirates"     "Turkey"                   "United States of America"
[41] "Vietnam [Viet Nam]"       "Serbia"                   "Albania"                  "Australia"               
[45] "Bahrain"                  "Canada"                   "Hong Kong"                "Israel"                  
[49] "Jordan"                   "Kuwait"                   "Macao"                    "Malaysia"                
table(data$EU_group)
< table of extent 0 >
head(data)
NA

Next we perform a sanity check:

data <- data %>%
  mutate(
    EU_group = case_when(
      Domicile_named_country %in% eu_real  ~ "Real_EU",
      Domicile_named_country %in% eu_synth ~ "Synthetic_EU"
    )
  )
table(data$EU_group)

     Real_EU Synthetic_EU 
         260          260

260/10 = 26 real European countries (excl. Ireland because it didn’t face the same restrictions (-1) and excluding UK for obvious reasons (-1)) and 260/10=26 synthetic countries (including Ireland because it is the most plausible counterfactual)

library(plm)

pdata_UCAS <- pdata.frame(data, index = c("Domicile_named_country", "Year"))

Saturated DID

S-DID design with DK errors

This chunk estimates a pooled difference-in-differences model using fixest::feols with Driscoll–Kraay (DK) standard errors. We convert the panel object to a plain data frame, ensure Year and country identifiers have the correct types, and define the post-treatment period (Post = 1 for years ≥ 2021) and treatment status (Treated = 1 for countries in eu_real). To allow heterogeneous post effects for each treated country while pooling all non-treated units, we build treated_factor: treated countries keep their own country label, while all controls are set to "CONTROL" (used as the reference category).

We then estimate three specifications: (1) a two-way fixed-effects model with country and year fixed effects, (2) a country fixed-effects-only model, and (3) a model without fixed effects, all using the same saturated interaction i(treated_factor, Post, ref = "CONTROL"). Model summaries are printed for comparison.

The interaction term i(treated_factor, Post, ref = "CONTROL") estimates a separate post-treatment effect for each treated country relative to the pooled control group. Formally, the specification can be written as

\[ \log(Applicants_{it}) = \alpha_i + \lambda_t + \sum_{c \in \mathcal{T}} \beta_c \left( \mathbf{1}\{i = c\} \times Post_t \right) + \varepsilon_{it}, \]

where \(i\) indexes countries and \(t\) indexes years, \(\alpha_i\) and \(\lambda_t\) denote country and year fixed effects, \(\mathcal{T}\) is the set of treated (EU) countries, and \(Post_t\) equals 1 for \(t \geq 2021\).
Each coefficient \(\beta_c\) captures the post-Brexit change in applications for treated country \(c\) relative to the pooled control group.

library(fixest)

# clean data 
df <- as.data.frame(pdata_UCAS)
df$Domicile_named_country <- as.character(df$Domicile_named_country)
df$Year <- as.integer(as.character(df$Year))

# treatment definition
df$Post    <- as.integer(df$Year >= 2021)                 # post-Brexit
df$Treated <- as.integer(df$Domicile_named_country %in% eu_real)

# pooled control category (NO NA on RHS!)
df$treated_factor <- ifelse(
  df$Treated == 1,
  df$Domicile_named_country,   # each treated country gets its own coefficient
  "CONTROL"                     # all unaffected countries pooled
)

m_sat_DK <- feols(
  log(Applicants) ~ i(treated_factor, Post, ref = "CONTROL") |
    Domicile_named_country + Year,
  data = df,
  panel.id = ~ Domicile_named_country + Year,
  vcov = "DK"
)

summary(m_sat_DK)
OLS estimation, Dep. Var.: log(Applicants)
Observations: 520
Fixed-effects: Domicile_named_country: 52,  Year: 10
Standard-errors: Driscoll-Kraay (L=1) 
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
RMSE: 0.179578     Adj. R2: 0.973907
                 Within R2: 0.802428
m_sat_DK1 <- feols(
  log(Applicants) ~ i(treated_factor, Post, ref = "CONTROL") |
    Domicile_named_country,
  data = df,
  panel.id = ~ Domicile_named_country + Year,
  vcov = "DK"
)

summary(m_sat_DK1)
OLS estimation, Dep. Var.: log(Applicants)
Observations: 520
Fixed-effects: Domicile_named_country: 52
Standard-errors: Driscoll-Kraay (L=1) 
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
RMSE: 0.197197     Adj. R2: 0.969176
                 Within R2: 0.820902
m_sat_DK2 <- feols(
  log(Applicants) ~ i(treated_factor, Post, ref = "CONTROL"),
  data = df,
  panel.id = ~ Domicile_named_country + Year,
  vcov = "DK"
)

summary(m_sat_DK2)
OLS estimation, Dep. Var.: log(Applicants)
Observations: 520
Standard-errors: Driscoll-Kraay (L=1) 
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
RMSE: 1.00887   Adj. R2: 0.276678

Event study

This chunk estimates an event-study difference-in-differences model using fixest. We define treatment status as \(EU_i=1\) for countries in eu_real and construct event time as \(\ell = t - 2020\), so \(\ell=0\) corresponds to year 2020. The regression uses i(event_time, EU, ref = 0) to estimate a full set of dynamic EU–control differences for each relative year \(\ell\), normalized to zero in the reference period \(\ell=0\). Country and year fixed effects are included, standard errors are clustered, and iplot() visualizes the estimated dynamic coefficients with \(\ell=0\) shown as the reference line. \[ \log(Applicants_{it})=\alpha_i+\lambda_t+\sum_{\ell\neq 0}\beta_{\ell}\Big(\mathbf{1}\{t-2020=\ell\}\times EU_i\Big)+\varepsilon_{it}, \]

where \(\beta_{\ell}\) traces the dynamic EU–control difference in year \(\ell\) relative to the baseline year \(\ell=0\) (2020).

Event graph

library(fixest)

df$Domicile_named_country <- as.character(df$Domicile_named_country)
df$Year <- as.integer(as.character(df$Year))

# treatment definition 
df$Post    <- as.integer(df$Year >= 2021)
df$EU      <- as.integer(df$Domicile_named_country %in% eu_real)

# event-time (ℓ = t − 2020)

df$event_time <- df$Year - 2020

# pooled EU vs control event-study DID 

m_event <- feols(
  log(Applicants) ~ i(event_time, EU, ref = 0) |
    Domicile_named_country + Year,
  data = df,
  panel.id = ~ Domicile_named_country + Year, 
  vcov="cluster"
)

summary(m_event)
OLS estimation, Dep. Var.: log(Applicants)
Observations: 520
Fixed-effects: Domicile_named_country: 52,  Year: 10
Standard-errors: Clustered (Domicile_named_country) 
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
RMSE: 0.239724     Adj. R2: 0.955258
                 Within R2: 0.647922
iplot(
  m_event,
  xlab = "Years relative to 2020",
  ylab = "Log difference in applications",
  ref.line = 0,
  main = "Event-study: EU vs synthetic control"
)


wald(m_event, c(
"event_time::-4:EU",
"event_time::-3:EU",
"event_time::-2:EU",
"event_time::-1:EU"
))
Wald test, H0: joint nullity of event_time::-4:EU, event_time::-3:EU, event_time::-2:EU and event_time::-1:EU
 stat = 1.83734, p-value = 0.120567, on 4 and 450 DoF, VCOV: Clustered (Domicile_named_country).

Interestingly, if we remove the clustering the following happens:

etable(m_event, vcov="iid")
                                   m_event
Dependent Var.:            log(Applicants)
                                          
EU x event_time = -4       0.1239 (0.1011)
EU x event_time = -3       0.0789 (0.1011)
EU x event_time = -2       0.0782 (0.1011)
EU x event_time = -1       0.0574 (0.1011)
EU x event_time = 1    -0.7958*** (0.1011)
EU x event_time = 2     -1.188*** (0.1011)
EU x event_time = 3     -1.294*** (0.1011)
EU x event_time = 4     -1.335*** (0.1011)
EU x event_time = 5     -1.374*** (0.1011)
Fixed-Effects:         -------------------
Domicile_named_country                 Yes
Year                                   Yes
______________________ ___________________
S.E. type                              IID
Observations                           520
R2                                 0.96121
Within R2                          0.64792
---
Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

(notice that ths standard errors are equal everywhere). Under iid (homoskedastic) errors, the event-time dummies will have the same variance in this design, and therefore identical standard errors. That follows directly from the mechanics of OLS. With iid errors, the variance of each coefficient depends only on

\[ Var(\hat{\delta}_l)=\sigma^2(X_l'X_l'^{-1}) \]

and the second moment of the regressor, and since each event-time dummy has identical support and variance after fixed effects, their estimated variances must be the same. To see this observe what happens once those dummies are explicitly defined to have heteroscedasticity consistent standard errors:

etable(m_event, vcov="hetero")
                                   m_event
Dependent Var.:            log(Applicants)
                                          
EU x event_time = -4       0.1239 (0.1016)
EU x event_time = -3       0.0789 (0.0938)
EU x event_time = -2       0.0782 (0.0867)
EU x event_time = -1       0.0574 (0.0834)
EU x event_time = 1    -0.7958*** (0.0832)
EU x event_time = 2     -1.188*** (0.0847)
EU x event_time = 3     -1.294*** (0.0978)
EU x event_time = 4     -1.335*** (0.1028)
EU x event_time = 5     -1.374*** (0.1066)
Fixed-Effects:         -------------------
Domicile_named_country                 Yes
Year                                   Yes
______________________ ___________________
S.E. type              Heteroskedast.-rob.
Observations                           520
R2                                 0.96121
Within R2                          0.64792
---
Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

Thats generally because of the sandwich estimator that identifies the SEs in robust se design:

\[ Vcov(\beta)=(X'X)^{-1}X'\Omega X(X'X)^{-1} \] where the \(\Omega =\diag(\varepsilon_it^2)\) which is going to create some variance so chill.

Placebo graph

This chunk implements a placebo event-study to test for spurious pre-treatment dynamics. A fake treatment year is set to 2018 and event time is defined as \(\ell = t - 2018\). The specification i(event_time_pl2018, EU, ref = -1) estimates EU–control differences for each relative year while normalizing the year immediately before the placebo treatment (\(\ell=-1\)) to zero. Country and year fixed effects are included, and standard errors are clustered at the country level. The resulting coefficients are plotted to visually assess whether significant effects appear around the placebo treatment date.

\[ \log(Applicants_{it}) = \alpha_i + \lambda_t + \sum_{\ell \neq -1} \beta_{\ell} \left(\mathbf{1}\{t-2018=\ell\} \times EU_i\right) + \varepsilon_{it}, \]

where the coefficients \(\beta_{\ell}\) capture placebo EU–control differences relative to the pre-placebo year \(\ell=-1\).

#placebo event time (fake Brexit in 2018)
df$event_time_pl2018 <- df$Year - 2018

m_placebo_2018 <- feols(
  log(Applicants) ~ i(event_time_pl2018, EU, ref = -1) |
    Domicile_named_country + Year,
  data = df,
  panel.id = ~ Domicile_named_country + Year,
  cluster=~Domicile_named_country
)

summary(m_placebo_2018)
OLS estimation, Dep. Var.: log(Applicants)
Observations: 520
Fixed-effects: Domicile_named_country: 52,  Year: 10
Standard-errors: Clustered (Domicile_named_country) 
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
RMSE: 0.239724     Adj. R2: 0.955258
                 Within R2: 0.647922
# plot
iplot(
  m_placebo_2018,
  xlab = "Years relative to fake Brexit (2018)",
  ylab = "Log difference in applications",
  ref.line = 0,
  main = "Placebo event study: fake Brexit in 2018"
)


# leads-only sample (pre-2021 only) 
df_pre <- subset(df, Year < 2021)

df_pre$event_time <- df_pre$Year - 2020

m_leads_only <- feols(
  log(Applicants) ~ i(event_time, EU, ref = 0) |
    Domicile_named_country + Year,
  data = df_pre,
  panel.id = ~ Domicile_named_country + Year,
  cluster = ~Domicile_named_country
)

summary(m_leads_only)
OLS estimation, Dep. Var.: log(Applicants)
Observations: 260
Fixed-effects: Domicile_named_country: 52,  Year: 5
Standard-errors: Clustered (Domicile_named_country) 
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
RMSE: 0.09846     Adj. R2: 0.989981
                Within R2: 0.040027
iplot(
  m_leads_only,
  xlab = "Years relative to 2020",
  ylab = "Log difference in applications",
  ref.line = 0,
  main = "Leads-only test without anticipation"
)


wald(m_leads_only, c(
"event_time::-4:EU",
"event_time::-3:EU",
"event_time::-2:EU",
"event_time::-1:EU"
))
Wald test, H0: joint nullity of event_time::-4:EU, event_time::-3:EU, event_time::-2:EU and event_time::-1:EU
 stat = 1.84456, p-value = 0.121742, on 4 and 200 DoF, VCOV: Clustered (Domicile_named_country).

Randomized treatment assignment

set.seed(123)

# Since now we need the entire sample we will pull the data again
data <- read_excel(
  "data_filtered.xlsx",
  sheet = "full_data"
)

# drop unwanted territories
data <- data %>%
  filter(!Domicile_named_country %in% c(
    "St Barthelemy", "St Martin (French part)", "Reunion",
    "Martinique", "Guadeloupe", "Aland Islands",
    "Cyprus (Not otherwise specified)",
    "French West Indies (Not In Use)",
    "Canary Islands", "French Guiana"
  ))

# keep only observations with complete data for variables used in the model
data <- data %>%
  filter(
    !is.na(Domicile_named_country),
    !is.na(Year),
    !is.na(Applicants)
  )

# keep only countries with complete panel over the observed year range

years_needed <- sort(unique(data$Year))

complete_countries <- data %>%
  distinct(Domicile_named_country, Year) %>%
  count(Domicile_named_country, name = "n_years") %>%
  filter(n_years == length(years_needed)) %>%
  pull(Domicile_named_country)

data <- data %>%
  filter(Domicile_named_country %in% complete_countries)

data<-data%>% filter(Domicile_named_country!="All")

# define placebo control pool: only non-EU countries remaining in the cleaned sample
control_pool <- setdiff(unique(data$Domicile_named_country), eu_real)

# sample placebo-treated countries from non-EU pool
fake_EU <- sample(control_pool, length(eu_real), replace = FALSE)

# assign placebo treatment and event time
data <- data %>%
  mutate(
    EU_placebo = as.integer(Domicile_named_country %in% fake_EU),
    event_time = Year - 2020
  )

# print placebo assignment table

assignment_table <- data.frame(
  country = sort(unique(data$Domicile_named_country))
) %>%
  mutate(
    group = ifelse(country %in% fake_EU, "Placebo treated", "Control")
  ) %>%
  arrange(desc(group), country)

print(assignment_table)

# counts by group
print(table(assignment_table$group))

        Control Placebo treated 
            157              26 
# estimate model
m_placebo_random <- feols(
  log(Applicants) ~ i(event_time, EU_placebo, ref = 0) |
    Domicile_named_country + Year,
  data = data,
  panel.id = ~ Domicile_named_country + Year,
  cluster = ~ Domicile_named_country
)

summary(m_placebo_random)
OLS estimation, Dep. Var.: log(Applicants)
Observations: 1,830
Fixed-effects: Domicile_named_country: 183,  Year: 10
Standard-errors: Clustered (Domicile_named_country) 
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
RMSE: 0.384803     Adj. R2: 0.961344
                 Within R2: 0.005064
iplot(
  m_placebo_random,
  xlab = "Years relative to 2020",
  ylab = "Log difference in applications",
  ref.line = 0,
  main = "Placebo with randomized treatment assignment"
)

Saturated event study

library(fixest)

df <- as.data.frame(pdata_UCAS)

# make sure these are plain base variables

df$Domicile_named_country <- as.character(df$Domicile_named_country)
df$Year <- as.integer(as.character(df$Year))

df$event_time <- df$Year - 2020

# Now create a dummy category for each 

df$Treated <- df$Domicile_named_country %in% eu_real

df$treated_factor <- ifelse(
  df$Treated,
  df$Domicile_named_country,
  "CONTROL"
)

df$treated_factor <- factor(df$treated_factor)

# sanity checks

levels(df$treated_factor)
 [1] "Austria"                 "Belgium"                 "Bulgaria"                "CONTROL"                 "Croatia"                
 [6] "Cyprus (European Union)" "Czech Republic"          "Denmark"                 "Estonia"                 "Finland"                
[11] "France"                  "Germany"                 "Greece"                  "Hungary"                 "Italy"                  
[16] "Latvia"                  "Lithuania"               "Luxembourg"              "Malta"                   "Netherlands"            
[21] "Poland"                  "Portugal"                "Romania"                 "Slovakia"                "Slovenia"               
[26] "Spain"                   "Sweden"                 
length(levels(df$treated_factor))
[1] 27
m_sat_event <- feols(
  log(Applicants) ~ i(event_time, treated_factor, ref = 0, ref2 = "CONTROL") |
    Domicile_named_country + Year,
  data = df,
  panel.id = ~Domicile_named_country + Year,
  cluster = ~Domicile_named_country
)
summary(m_sat_event)
OLS estimation, Dep. Var.: log(Applicants)
Observations: 520
Fixed-effects: Domicile_named_country: 52,  Year: 10
Standard-errors: Clustered (Domicile_named_country) 
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
RMSE: 0.13856     Adj. R2: 0.970105
                Within R2: 0.882378
library(broom)
library(dplyr)
library(stringr)
library(ggplot2)

plot_df <- broom::tidy(m_sat_event) %>%
  filter(str_detect(term, "^event_time::")) %>%
  mutate(
    event_time = as.integer(str_extract(term, "-?\\d+(?=:)")),
    country = str_extract(term, "(?<=treated_factor::).*"),
    conf.low = estimate - 1.96 * std.error,
    conf.high = estimate + 1.96 * std.error
  ) %>%
  filter(!is.na(country), country != "CONTROL")

ggplot(plot_df, aes(x = event_time, y = estimate)) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0, linetype = "dotted") +
  geom_point(size = 1.6) +
  geom_line() +
  geom_errorbar(
    aes(ymin = conf.low, ymax = conf.high),
    width = 0.15
  ) +
  facet_wrap(~ country, scales = "free_y") +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(
    x = "Years relative to 2020",
    y = "Estimated log effect",
    title = "Saturated DID event study by country"
  )

D-El from S-DID compared with mechanical and fee based

library(dplyr)
library(ggplot2)
library(broom)

# 1. Brexit tuition shock

fee_pre  <- 9250
fee_post <- 22200

x <- log(fee_post/fee_pre)                     # log price change
pct_fee_change <- (fee_post - fee_pre) / fee_pre   # ≈ 1.40

# 2. Elasticities from DiD regression

did_df <- tidy(m_sat_DK, conf.int = TRUE) %>%
  filter(grepl("^treated_factor::", term)) %>%
  mutate(
    country = gsub("^treated_factor::", "", term),
    country = gsub(":Post$", "", country),

    beta = estimate,   # Δ log Q (DiD semi-elasticity)

    # 1) DiD quantity change (log change)
    log_change = beta,
    conf_low_log  = conf.low,
    conf_high_log = conf.high,

    # 2) DiD implied % quantity change
    pct_change_Q = exp(beta) - 1,

    # 3) Arc elasticity from DiD
    eta_arc_did = (exp(beta) - 1) / pct_fee_change,

    # 4) Structural log elasticity
    eta_log = beta / x
  ) %>%
  select(country, log_change, conf_low_log, conf_high_log,
         pct_change_Q, eta_arc_did, eta_log)


# 3. Mechanical fee-based elasticity

fee_df <- df %>%
  filter(Domicile_named_country %in% eu_real) %>%
  group_by(Domicile_named_country) %>%
  summarise(
    app_pre  = mean(Applicants[Year == 2020], na.rm = TRUE),
    app_post = mean(Applicants[Year == 2021], na.rm = TRUE),

    # η_mechanical = %ΔQ / %ΔP
    eta_mechanical = ((app_post - app_pre) / app_pre) / pct_fee_change,
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  rename(country = Domicile_named_country)

# 4. Merge datasets

plot_df <- did_df %>%
  left_join(fee_df, by = "country") %>%
  arrange(eta_log) %>%
  mutate(
    country = factor(country, levels = country),

    # small horizontal offsets so points don’t overlap
    eta_arc_shift  = eta_arc_did + 0.04,
    eta_mech_shift = eta_mechanical + 0.08
  )

# 5. Plot

ggplot(plot_df, aes(y = country)) +

  # zero line
  geom_vline(
    xintercept = 0,
    linetype = "dashed",
    color = "grey70",
    linewidth = 0.35
  ) +

  # confidence intervals (only structural elasticity has SEs)
  geom_errorbarh(
    aes(xmin = conf_low_log, xmax = conf_high_log),
    height = 0.16,
    linewidth = 0.4,
    color = "grey45"
  ) +

  # connectors between estimates
  geom_segment(
    aes(x = eta_log, xend = eta_arc_shift, yend = country),
    color = "grey80",
    linewidth = 0.3
  ) +
  geom_segment(
    aes(x = eta_arc_shift, xend = eta_mech_shift, yend = country),
    color = "grey85",
    linewidth = 0.3
  ) +

  # (A) Structural elasticity (log derivative)
  geom_point(
    aes(x = eta_log),
    shape = 21,
    size = 2.8,
    fill = "#2C6DB2",
    color = "black",
    stroke = 0.25
  ) +

  # (B) DiD arc elasticity
  geom_point(
    aes(x = eta_arc_shift),
    shape = 21,
    size = 2.6,
    fill = "#8FBCE6",
    color = "black",
    stroke = 0.25
  ) +

  # (C) Mechanical fee elasticity
  geom_point(
    aes(x = eta_mech_shift),
    shape = 21,
    size = 2.6,
    fill = "white",
    color = "black",
    stroke = 0.25
  ) +

  labs(
    title = "Decomposition of Post-Brexit Demand Elasticities",
    subtitle = "Dark: structural log elasticity; Light: DiD arc elasticity; Hollow: fee-based elasticity",
    x = "Demand elasticity",
    y = "Country"
  ) +

  theme_classic(base_size = 13) +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face="bold", size=15, margin=margin(b=6)),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size=11, margin=margin(b=10)),
    axis.text.y = element_text(size=10),
    axis.line = element_line(linewidth=0.35),
    plot.margin = margin(10,12,10,10)
  )

NA
NA

S-DID design DE with COVID and Post-COVID coefficients

df$Post_COVID <- as.integer(df$Year >= 2024)
df$Post <- as.integer(df$Year %in% c(2021,2023) ) 

#pooled control category (NO NA on RHS!)

df$treated_factor <- ifelse(
  df$Treated == 1,
  df$Domicile_named_country,   # each treated country gets its own coefficient
  "CONTROL"                     # all unaffected countries pooled
)

library(fixest)

m_sat_DK_2 <- feols(
  log(Applicants) ~
    i(treated_factor, Post, ref = "CONTROL") +
    i(treated_factor, Post_COVID, ref = "CONTROL") |
    Domicile_named_country + Year,
  data = df,
  panel.id = ~ Domicile_named_country + Year,
  vcov = "DK"
)

summary(m_sat_DK_2)
OLS estimation, Dep. Var.: log(Applicants)
Observations: 520
Fixed-effects: Domicile_named_country: 52,  Year: 10
Standard-errors: Driscoll-Kraay (L=1) 
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
RMSE: 0.267096     Adj. R2: 0.938588
                 Within R2: 0.562928
library(dplyr)
library(ggplot2)
library(tidyr)
library(broom)
library(stringr)

# 1) extract coefficients
coef_long <- tidy(m_sat_DK_2) %>%
  filter(str_detect(term, "^treated_factor::")) %>%
  mutate(
    period = case_when(
      str_detect(term, "Post_COVID$") ~ "Post-COVID",
      str_detect(term, "Post$") ~ "Post-Brexit",
      TRUE ~ NA_character_
    ),
    country = term %>%
      str_remove("^treated_factor::") %>%
      str_remove(":(Post|Post_COVID)$"),
    elasticity = estimate
  ) %>%
  filter(!is.na(period))

# 2) wide version (for ordering + segments)
plot_df2 <- coef_long %>%
  select(country, period, elasticity) %>%
  pivot_wider(names_from = period, values_from = elasticity) %>%
  arrange(desc(`Post-COVID`)) %>%
  mutate(country = factor(country, levels = unique(country)))

# 3) long version (for legend + points) 
plot_df_long <- plot_df2 %>%
  pivot_longer(
    cols = c(`Post-Brexit`, `Post-COVID`),
    names_to = "period",
    values_to = "elasticity"
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    period = factor(
      period,
      levels = c("Post-Brexit", "Post-COVID"),
      labels = c(
        "2021 tuition shock (COVID+Brexit)",
        "Persistent response (post-COVID, Brexit only)"
      )
    )
  )

# 4) plot
ggplot(plot_df_long, aes(y = country)) +

  # connecting lines (use wide data)
  geom_segment(
    data = plot_df2,
    aes(
      x = `Post-Brexit`,
      xend = `Post-COVID`,
      yend = country
    ),
    color = "grey75",
    linewidth = 0.6
  ) +

  # points with proper legend
  geom_point(
    aes(x = elasticity, color = period),
    size = 2.6
  ) +

  geom_vline(
    xintercept = 0,
    linetype = "dashed",
    color = "grey70",
    linewidth = 0.35
  ) +

  scale_color_manual(
    values = c("#4C78A8", "#E45756"),
    name = NULL
  ) +

  labs(
    title = "Change in Demand Elasticities After Brexit",
    subtitle = "Immediate tuition shock vs persistent post-COVID response",
    x = "Demand elasticity",
    y = ""
  ) +

  theme_classic(base_size = 13) +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 15),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 11),
    axis.text.y = element_text(size = 10),
    legend.position = "top",
    legend.text = element_text(size = 10)
  )

Robustness

Prepare the datasets

data <- read_excel(
  "data_filtered.xlsx", 
  sheet = "full_data"
)

data <- data %>%
  filter(!Domicile_named_country %in% c(
    "St Barthelemy", "St Martin (French part)", "Reunion",
    "Martinique", "Guadeloupe", "Aland Islands",
    "Cyprus (Not otherwise specified)",
    "French West Indies (Not In Use)",
    "Canary Islands", "French Guiana"
  ))


data_standard <- data %>%
  filter(Domicile_named_country %in% c(eu_real, eu_synth)) %>%
  mutate(
    EU = as.integer(Domicile_named_country %in% eu_real),
    control_group = "Synthetic"
  )

#very very poor countries
synth_poor <- c(
  "Pakistan", "Nepal", "Bangladesh", "Ethiopia",
  "Uganda", "Tanzania, United Republic of", "Rwanda", "Mozambique",
  "Malawi", "Sierra Leone",
  "Ghana", "Nigeria", "Kenya", "Zimbabwe"
)
data_poor <- data %>%
  filter(Domicile_named_country %in% c(eu_real, synth_poor)) %>%
  mutate(
    EU = as.integer(Domicile_named_country %in% eu_real),
    control_group = "Very_Poor"
  )
#very very rich countries

synth_rich <- c(
  "United States of America", "Canada", "Australia", "Norway",
  "Switzerland", "Singapore", "Qatar", "United Arab Emirates",
  "Kuwait", "Saudi Arabia", "Israel", "Japan",
  "Hong Kong"
)

data_rich <- data %>%
  filter(Domicile_named_country %in% c(eu_real, synth_rich)) %>%
  mutate(
    EU = as.integer(Domicile_named_country %in% eu_real),
    control_group = "Very_Rich"
  )

data_rich<-data_rich[data_rich$Year>2015,]
#OECD only 
oecd <- c(
  "Australia", "Canada", "Chile",
  "Colombia",
  "Iceland", "Israel", "Japan",
  "Korea, Republic of", "Mexico",
  "New Zealand", "Norway",
  "Switzerland", "Turkey",
  "United States of America"
)
data_oecd <- data %>%
  filter(Domicile_named_country %in% c(eu_real, oecd)) %>%
  mutate(
    EU = as.integer(Domicile_named_country %in% eu_real),
    control_group = "OECD"
  )

data_oecd<-data_oecd[data_oecd$Year>2015,]
library(dplyr)
library(fixest)

# --- core helper: filter to treated + a chosen control set, then run pooled DiD ---

run_pooled_did <- function(df, treated_vec, control_vec,
                           y = "Applicants",
                           post_year = 2021,
                           vcov_type = "DK") {

  df2 <- df %>%
    filter(Domicile_named_country %in% c(treated_vec, control_vec)) %>%
    mutate(
      EU   = as.integer(Domicile_named_country %in% treated_vec),
      Post = as.integer(Year >= post_year)
    )

  m <- feols(
    as.formula(paste0(
      "log(", y, ") ~ EU:Post | Domicile_named_country + Year"
    )),
    data = df2,
    panel.id = ~Domicile_named_country + Year,
    vcov = vcov_type
  )

  return(list(model = m, data = df2))
}

controls_list <- list(
  "Original synthetic" = eu_synth,
  "Very poor controls" = synth_poor,
  "Very rich controls" = synth_rich,
  "OECD controls"      = setdiff(oecd, eu_real)  # keep OECD but remove treated EU from control pool
)

fits <- lapply(names(controls_list), function(nm) {
  run_pooled_did(
    df = data[data$Year>2015,],
    treated_vec = eu_real,
    control_vec = controls_list[[nm]],
    y = "Applicants",
    post_year = 2021, 
    vcov_type = "DK"
  )$model
})

names(fits) <- names(controls_list)
get_ate <- function(m) {
  ct <- coeftable(m)

  data.frame(
    ATE = ct["EU:Post", "Estimate"],
    SE  = ct["EU:Post", "Std. Error"],
    t   = ct["EU:Post", "t value"],
    p   = ct["EU:Post", "Pr(>|t|)"],
    N   = nobs(m),
    R2_within = as.numeric(fitstat(m, "wr2"))
  )
}

ATE_table <- do.call(rbind, lapply(fits, get_ate))
ATE_table$Spec <- names(fits)
ATE_table <- ATE_table[, c("Spec","ATE","SE","t","p","N","R2_within")]

print(ATE_table)
NA

TWFE DIDs for different control groups


ATE_table <- ATE_table[
  match(
    c("Very poor controls",
      "Original synthetic",
      "Very rich controls",
      "OECD controls"),
    ATE_table$Spec
  ),
]

# sanity check
stopifnot(!any(is.na(ATE_table$Spec)))

# ---- confidence intervals ----
ATE_table$lower <- ATE_table$ATE - 1.96 * ATE_table$SE
ATE_table$upper <- ATE_table$ATE + 1.96 * ATE_table$SE

# ---- numeric y positions ----
y_pos <- seq_len(nrow(ATE_table))

library(ggplot2)

ggplot(ATE_table, aes(x = ATE, y = Spec)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_errorbarh(aes(xmin = lower, xmax = upper), height = 0.2) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed") +
  xlab("ATE (log points)") +
  ylab("")

Bootstrapped ATE

library(fixest)
library(dplyr)

set.seed(123)

eu_countries     <- intersect(unique(data$Domicile_named_country), eu_real)
non_eu_countries <- setdiff(unique(data$Domicile_named_country), eu_real)

B <- 1000
boot_ATE <- rep(NA_real_, B)

for (b in 1:B) {

  boot_eu  <- sample(eu_countries, length(eu_countries), replace = TRUE)
  boot_neu <- sample(non_eu_countries, length(non_eu_countries), replace = TRUE)

  boot_countries <- c(boot_eu, boot_neu)

  df_b <- data %>%
    filter(Domicile_named_country %in% boot_countries) %>%
    mutate(
      EU   = as.integer(Domicile_named_country %in% eu_real),
      Post = as.integer(Year >= 2021)
    )

m_b <- suppressMessages(
  suppressWarnings(
    feols(
      log(Applicants) ~ EU : Post |
        Domicile_named_country + Year,
      data = df_b,
      fixef.rm = "perfect"
    )
  )
)

  if ("EU:Post" %in% names(coef(m_b))) {
    boot_ATE[b] <- coef(m_b)["EU:Post"]
  }
}

boot_ATE_clean <- boot_ATE[!is.na(boot_ATE)]

# diagnostics
length(boot_ATE_clean) / B    # should be high (e.g. > 0.9)
[1] 1
# inference
quantile(boot_ATE_clean, c(0.025, 0.975))
     2.5%     97.5% 
-1.412975 -1.113521 
sd(boot_ATE_clean)
[1] 0.07822566
mean(boot_ATE_clean)
[1] -1.269757
#Option 1
hist(
  boot_ATE_clean,
  breaks = 40,
  main = "Stratified cluster bootstrap: Brexit ATE",
  xlab = "EU × Post coefficient",
  freq=FALSE
)
abline(v = 0, lty = 2)
abline(v = mean(boot_ATE_clean), col = "red")



#Option 2
lattice::densityplot(
  ~ boot_ATE_clean,
  plot.points = FALSE,
  auto.key = list(columns = 2),
  lwd = 2, 
  xlab = "EU × Post coefficient",
  main = "Stratified cluster bootstrap: Brexit ATE"
)

Callaway–Sant’Anna DR for different controls

library(dplyr)
library(did)

run_cs <- function(dat, eu_vec = eu_real){

  dat2 <- dat %>%
    mutate(
      EU = as.integer(Domicile_named_country %in% eu_vec),
      id = as.numeric(factor(Domicile_named_country)),
      t  = Year,
      g  = ifelse(EU == 1, 2021, 0),
      log_app = log(Applicants)   # use log1p(Applicants) if you have zeros
    )

  att <- att_gt(
    yname  = "log_app",
    tname  = "t",
    idname = "id",est_method = "dr",
    gname  = "g",
    data   = dat2,
    panel  = TRUE
  )

  list(
    simple  = aggte(att, type = "simple"),
    dynamic = aggte(att, type = "dynamic")
  )
}

# --- run on each synthetic donor pool ---data_standard
res_st <- run_cs(data_standard[data_standard$Year>2015,])
res_poor <- run_cs(data_poor[data_poor$Year>2015,])
res_rich <- run_cs(data_rich)
res_oecd <- run_cs(data_oecd)

# --- print overall ATT ---
summary(res_st$simple)

Call:
aggte(MP = att, type = "simple")

Reference: Callaway, Brantly and Pedro H.C. Sant'Anna.  "Difference-in-Differences with Multiple Time Periods." Journal of Econometrics, Vol. 225, No. 2, pp. 200-230, 2021. <https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jeconom.2020.12.001>, <https://arxiv.org/abs/1803.09015> 


---
Signif. codes: `*' confidence band does not cover 0

Control Group:  Never Treated,  Anticipation Periods:  0
Estimation Method:  Doubly Robust
summary(res_poor$simple)

Call:
aggte(MP = att, type = "simple")

Reference: Callaway, Brantly and Pedro H.C. Sant'Anna.  "Difference-in-Differences with Multiple Time Periods." Journal of Econometrics, Vol. 225, No. 2, pp. 200-230, 2021. <https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jeconom.2020.12.001>, <https://arxiv.org/abs/1803.09015> 


---
Signif. codes: `*' confidence band does not cover 0

Control Group:  Never Treated,  Anticipation Periods:  0
Estimation Method:  Doubly Robust
summary(res_rich$simple)

Call:
aggte(MP = att, type = "simple")

Reference: Callaway, Brantly and Pedro H.C. Sant'Anna.  "Difference-in-Differences with Multiple Time Periods." Journal of Econometrics, Vol. 225, No. 2, pp. 200-230, 2021. <https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jeconom.2020.12.001>, <https://arxiv.org/abs/1803.09015> 


---
Signif. codes: `*' confidence band does not cover 0

Control Group:  Never Treated,  Anticipation Periods:  0
Estimation Method:  Doubly Robust
summary(res_oecd$simple)

Call:
aggte(MP = att, type = "simple")

Reference: Callaway, Brantly and Pedro H.C. Sant'Anna.  "Difference-in-Differences with Multiple Time Periods." Journal of Econometrics, Vol. 225, No. 2, pp. 200-230, 2021. <https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jeconom.2020.12.001>, <https://arxiv.org/abs/1803.09015> 


---
Signif. codes: `*' confidence band does not cover 0

Control Group:  Never Treated,  Anticipation Periods:  0
Estimation Method:  Doubly Robust
# --- plot event-study style ATT ---
ggdid(res_st$dynamic)  

ggdid(res_poor$dynamic)  # poor donor pool

ggdid(res_rich$dynamic)  # rich donor pool

ggdid(res_oecd$dynamic)  # OECD donor pool

Omitted-variable sensitivity - sensemakr (Cinelli–Hazlett) {#sec-omitted-variable-sensitivity–sensemakr-(cinelli–hazlett}

library(sensemakr)

df <- df %>%
  mutate(
    Post_2021 = Post + Post_COVID,
    EU = ifelse(Domicile_named_country %in% eu_real, 1, 0)
  )

m <- lm(
  log(Applicants) ~ EU:Post_2021 + as.factor(Domicile_named_country) + as.factor(Year),
  data = df
)


summary(m) # shows the estimated coefficient

Call:
lm(formula = log(Applicants) ~ EU:Post_2021 + as.factor(Domicile_named_country) + 
    as.factor(Year), data = df)

Residuals:
     Min       1Q   Median       3Q      Max 
-1.04993 -0.19131 -0.02188  0.21224  1.27839 

Coefficients:
                                                          Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept)                                                4.83399    0.11372  42.506  < 2e-16 ***
as.factor(Domicile_named_country)Australia                 1.70635    0.14769  11.554  < 2e-16 ***

---
Signif. codes:  0 ‘***’ 0.001 ‘**’ 0.01 ‘*’ 0.05 ‘.’ 0.1 ‘ ’ 1

Residual standard error: 0.3302 on 458 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.9352,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.9265 
F-statistic: 108.3 on 61 and 458 DF,  p-value: < 2.2e-16
sensemakr(m, treatment = "EU:Post_2021")
Sensitivity Analysis to Unobserved Confounding

Model Formula: log(Applicants) ~ EU:Post_2021 + as.factor(Domicile_named_country) + 
    as.factor(Year)

Null hypothesis: q = 1 and reduce = TRUE 

Unadjusted Estimates of ' EU:Post_2021 ':
  Coef. estimate: -1.05806 
  Standard Error: 0.05912 
  t-value: -17.8961 

Sensitivity Statistics:
  Partial R2 of treatment with outcome: 0.41152 
  Robustness Value, q = 1 : 0.55674 
  Robustness Value, q = 1 alpha = 0.05 : 0.51718 

For more information, check summary.

To assess the robustness of the estimated treatment effect to omitted variable bias, we apply the sensemakr framework. This approach evaluates how strong an unobserved confounder would need to be, in terms of its residual association with both the treatment and the outcome, to alter the substantive conclusions. We construct the sensitivity object. Let \(U\) denote a hypothetical omitted confounder. The sensitivity analysis evaluates the partial \(R^2\) of \(U\) with the outcome conditional on the treatment and observed covariates,

\[ R^2_{Y \sim U \mid X,D} \]

and the partial \(R^2\) of \(U\) with the treatment conditional on observed covariates,

\[ R^2_{D \sim U \mid X} \]

where \(D = EU_i \times Post2021_t\) is the treatment interaction and \(X\) includes the country and year fixed effects.

The robustness value summarizes the minimum strength that an omitted confounder must have, jointly with the treatment and outcome, to overturn the inference. This quantity represents the smallest combination of \(R^2_{Y \sim U \mid X,D}\) and \(R^2_{D \sim U \mid X}\) that would render the treatment effect statistically insignificant.

We also compute the robustness value required to reduce the point estimate to zero:

Setting \(q = 1\) asks how strong an omitted confounder would need to be to fully explain away the estimated effect, i.e.

\[ \hat{\beta}_1^{adjusted} = 0 \]

The sensitivity plot visualizes combinations of partial \(R^2\) values for a hypothetical omitted confounder that would be sufficient to attenuate the estimate or its statistical significance.

library(sensemakr)

m <- lm(log(Applicants) ~ EU:Post_2021+as.factor(Domicile_named_country)+as.factor(Year), data = df)

# Sensitivity object (optional, for plots etc.)
s <- sensemakr(m, treatment = "EU:Post_2021")

# Robustness value: pass the MODEL (m), not s
robustness_value(m, treatment = "EU:Post_2021")

plot(s)

summary(summary(sensemakr(m, treatment = "EU:Post_2021")))
Sensitivity Analysis to Unobserved Confounding

Model Formula: log(Applicants) ~ EU:Post_2021 + as.factor(Domicile_named_country) + 
    as.factor(Year)

Null hypothesis: q = 1 and reduce = TRUE 
-- This means we are considering biases that reduce the absolute value of the current estimate.
-- The null hypothesis deemed problematic is H0:tau = 0 

Unadjusted Estimates of 'EU:Post_2021': 
  Coef. estimate: -1.0581 
  Standard Error: 0.0591 
  t-value (H0:tau = 0): -17.8961 

Sensitivity Statistics:
  Partial R2 of treatment with outcome: 0.4115 
  Robustness Value, q = 1: 0.5567 
  Robustness Value, q = 1, alpha = 0.05: 0.5172 

Verbal interpretation of sensitivity statistics:

-- Partial R2 of the treatment with the outcome: an extreme confounder (orthogonal to the covariates) that explains 100% of the residual variance of the outcome, would need to explain at least 41.15% of the residual variance of the treatment to fully account for the observed estimated effect.

-- Robustness Value, q = 1: unobserved confounders (orthogonal to the covariates) that explain more than 55.67% of the residual variance of both the treatment and the outcome are strong enough to bring the point estimate to 0 (a bias of 100% of the original estimate). Conversely, unobserved confounders that do not explain more than 55.67% of the residual variance of both the treatment and the outcome are not strong enough to bring the point estimate to 0.

-- Robustness Value, q = 1, alpha = 0.05: unobserved confounders (orthogonal to the covariates) that explain more than 51.72% of the residual variance of both the treatment and the outcome are strong enough to bring the estimate to a range where it is no longer 'statistically different' from 0 (a bias of 100% of the original estimate), at the significance level of alpha = 0.05. Conversely, unobserved confounders that do not explain more than 51.72% of the residual variance of both the treatment and the outcome are not strong enough to bring the estimate to a range where it is no longer 'statistically different' from 0, at the significance level of alpha = 0.05.

Length  Class   Mode 
     0   NULL   NULL 
ovb_minimal_reporting(s, format = "latex")

\hline 
\textit{EU:Post_2021} & -1.058 & 0.059 & -17.896 & 41.2\% & 55.7\% & 51.7\% \\

df = 458 & & \multicolumn{5}{r}{ }\end{tabular}
\end{table}

ATE wealth heterogeneity

Constructing the database


rm(list = ls())            # remove all objects
gc()                       # garbage collection
           used  (Mb) gc trigger  (Mb) max used  (Mb)
Ncells  4730455 252.7    7905212 422.2  7905212 422.2
Vcells 10715082  81.8   17824054 136.0 17824011 136.0
data <- read_excel(
  "data_filtered.xlsx", 
  sheet = "full_data"
)

data <- data %>%
  filter(!Domicile_named_country %in% c(
    "St Barthelemy", "St Martin (French part)", "Reunion",
    "Martinique", "Guadeloupe", "Aland Islands",
    "Cyprus (Not otherwise specified)",
    "French West Indies (Not In Use)",
    "Canary Islands", "French Guiana"
  ))

eu_real <- c(
  "Austria", "Belgium", "Bulgaria", "Croatia", "Cyprus (European Union)",
  "Czech Republic", "Denmark", "Estonia", "Finland", "France",
  "Germany", "Greece", "Hungary",  "Italy",
  "Latvia", "Lithuania", "Luxembourg", "Malta", "Netherlands",
  "Poland", "Portugal", "Romania", "Slovakia", "Slovenia",
  "Spain", "Sweden"
)

eu_synth <- c("Australia", "Saudi Arabia", "Russian Federation", "Peru", "Kuwait", "Bahrain","Singapore","Switzerland", "Hong Kong", "United States of America",  "Thailand", "Canada", "Mauritius", "Qatar", "Norway", "Israel", "Malaysia", "Jordan", "Albania", "Serbia", "Ireland", "Jersey", "Macao","United Arab Emirates", "Vietnam [Viet Nam]", "Turkey")


data <- data %>%
  filter(Domicile_named_country %in% c(eu_real, eu_synth))

data <- data %>%
  mutate(
    real_EU  = ifelse(Domicile_named_country %in% eu_real, 1, 0),
    synth_EU = ifelse(Domicile_named_country %in% eu_synth, 1, 0),
    EU_group = case_when(
      Domicile_named_country %in% eu_real ~ "Real_EU",
      Domicile_named_country %in% eu_synth ~ "Synthetic_EU",
      TRUE ~ "Other"
    )
  )

library(WDI)
library(WDI)
library(dplyr)
library(countrycode)

# years actually present in your data
yr_min <- min(data$Year, na.rm = TRUE)
yr_max <- min(max(data$Year, na.rm = TRUE), 2024)  # cap to available WB years if needed

# only pull countries that are in your dataset
iso_list <- data %>%
  mutate(iso3c = countrycode(Domicile_named_country, "country.name", "iso3c")) %>%
  filter(!is.na(iso3c)) %>%
  distinct(iso3c) %>%
  pull(iso3c)

wb_indicators <- c(
  GDPpc = "NY.GDP.PCAP.KD",      # GDP per capita, constant USD
  Pop15_65 = "SP.POP.1564.TO",
  YouthUnempl = "SL.UEM.1524.ZS"
)

controls <- WDI(
  country = iso_list,
  indicator = wb_indicators,
  start = yr_min,
  end = yr_max,
  extra = TRUE
) %>%
  transmute(
    iso3c,
    Year = year,
    GDPpc,
    Pop15_65,
    YouthUnempl
  )

data_controls <- data %>%
  mutate(iso3c = countrycode(Domicile_named_country, "country.name", "iso3c")) %>%
  left_join(controls, by = c("iso3c", "Year"))

## ATE wealth heterogeneity FO

library(fixest)
library(dplyr)
library(modelsummary)

# Base Panel Dataset
data_panel <- data_controls %>%
  mutate(
    log_app = log(Applicants),
    EU = as.numeric(Domicile_named_country %in% eu_real),
    Post = as.numeric(Year >= 2021),
    did = EU * Post
  ) %>%
  filter(!is.na(log_app))

# M1 — Clean baseline DiD
m1 <- feols(
  log_app ~ did | Domicile_named_country + Year,
  data = data_panel,
  panel.id = ~Domicile_named_country + Year,
  vcov = "DK"
)

# M2 — Add GDP
m2 <- feols(
  log_app ~ did + log(GDPpc) | Domicile_named_country + Year,
  data = data_panel,
  panel.id = ~Domicile_named_country + Year,
  vcov = "DK"
)

# M4 — Add Pop15_65
m4 <- feols(
  log_app ~ did + log(GDPpc) + log(Pop15_65) | Domicile_named_country + Year,
  data = data_panel,
  panel.id = ~Domicile_named_country + Year,
  vcov = "DK"
)

# M5 — Add YouthUnempl + Pop15_65
m5 <- feols(
  log_app ~ did + log(GDPpc) + YouthUnempl + log(Pop15_65) | Domicile_named_country + Year,
  data = data_panel,
  panel.id = ~Domicile_named_country + Year,
  vcov = "DK"
)

# M6 — Fully interacted structural channels
m6 <- feols(
  log_app ~ did * (log(GDPpc) + log(Pop15_65) + YouthUnempl) | Domicile_named_country + Year,
  data = data_panel,
  panel.id = ~Domicile_named_country + Year,
  vcov = "DK"
)

modelsummary(
  list(
    "M1" = m1,
    "M2" = m2,
    "M4" = m4,
    "M5" = m5,
    "M6" = m6
  ),
  stars = TRUE,
  output = "default"
)

FE: Domicile_named_country X X X X X
FE: Year X X X X X 
m_pois1 <- fepois(
  Applicants ~ did | Domicile_named_country + Year,
  data = data_panel,
  panel.id = ~Domicile_named_country + Year,
  vcov = "DK"
)
m_pois2 <- fepois(
  Applicants ~ did + log(GDPpc) + log(Pop15_65) + YouthUnempl |
  Domicile_named_country + Year,
  data = data_panel,
  panel.id = ~Domicile_named_country + Year,
  vcov = "DK"
)
m_pois3 <- fepois(
  Applicants ~ did*(log(GDPpc) + log(Pop15_65) + YouthUnempl) |
  Domicile_named_country + Year,
  data = data_panel,
  panel.id = ~Domicile_named_country + Year,
  vcov = "DK"
)

modelsummary(
  list(
    "M1" = m_pois1,
    "M2" = m_pois2, 
    "M3"=m_pois3
  ),
  stars = TRUE,
  output = "default"
)

R2 Adj. 0.952 0.964 0.969
R2 Within 0.590 0.670 0.718
R2 Within Adj. 0.590 0.670 0.718
AIC 39900.2 25735.4 22015.0
BIC 40163.9 25995.5 22287.5
RMSE 382.35 325.10 299.70
Std.Errors Driscoll-Kraay (L=1) Driscoll-Kraay (L=1) Driscoll-Kraay (L=1)
FE: Domicile_named_country X X X
FE: Year X X X 
library(fixest)
library(dplyr)
library(modelsummary)

# ----------------------------
# 1. build clean analysis data
# ----------------------------
df_sat <- data_panel %>%
  mutate(
    Domicile_named_country = as.character(Domicile_named_country),
    Year = as.integer(Year),

    Applicants = as.numeric(Applicants),
    GDPpc = as.numeric(GDPpc),
    YouthUnempl = as.numeric(YouthUnempl),

    # handle either Pop15_64 or Pop15_65 if naming differs
    Pop_working_age =as.numeric(Pop15_65),

    log_app = log(Applicants),

    # treatment
    Post = as.integer(Year >= 2021),
    Treated = as.integer(Domicile_named_country %in% eu_real),

    # pooled control group for saturated DiD
    treated_factor = ifelse(
      Treated == 1,
      Domicile_named_country,
      "CONTROL"
    ),

    # logs of controls
    log_GDPpc = log(GDPpc),
    log_Pop_working_age = log(Pop_working_age)
  ) %>%
  filter(
    !is.na(log_app),
    !is.na(Post),
    !is.na(treated_factor)
  )

# optional check
table(df_sat$treated_factor == "CONTROL")

FALSE  TRUE 
  260   260 
sort(unique(df_sat$treated_factor))[1:10]
 [1] "Austria"                 "Belgium"                 "Bulgaria"                "CONTROL"                 "Croatia"                
 [6] "Cyprus (European Union)" "Czech Republic"          "Denmark"                 "Estonia"                 "Finland"                
# ----------------------------
# 2. saturated DiD models
# ----------------------------

# S1: saturated DiD only
s1 <- feols(
  log_app ~ i(treated_factor, Post, ref = "CONTROL") |
    Domicile_named_country + Year,
  data = df_sat,
  panel.id = ~ Domicile_named_country + Year,
  vcov = "DK"
)

# S2: add GDP
s2 <- feols(
  log_app ~ i(treated_factor, Post, ref = "CONTROL") + log_GDPpc |
    Domicile_named_country + Year,
  data = df_sat %>% filter(!is.na(log_GDPpc)),
  panel.id = ~ Domicile_named_country + Year,
  vcov = "DK"
)

# S3: add GDP + working-age population
s3 <- feols(
  log_app ~ i(treated_factor, Post, ref = "CONTROL") +
    log_GDPpc + log_Pop_working_age |
    Domicile_named_country + Year,
  data = df_sat %>% filter(!is.na(log_GDPpc), !is.na(log_Pop_working_age)),
  panel.id = ~ Domicile_named_country + Year,
  vcov = "DK"
)

# S4: add GDP + working-age population + youth unemployment
s4 <- feols(
  log_app ~ i(treated_factor, Post, ref = "CONTROL") +
    log_GDPpc + log_Pop_working_age + YouthUnempl |
    Domicile_named_country + Year,
  data = df_sat %>% filter(
    !is.na(log_GDPpc),
    !is.na(log_Pop_working_age),
    !is.na(YouthUnempl)
  ),
  panel.id = ~ Domicile_named_country + Year,
  vcov = "DK"
)
modelsummary(
  list(
    "S1 Saturated DiD" = s1,
    "S2 + GDPpc" = s2,
    "S3 + GDPpc + Pop" = s3,
    "S4 + GDPpc + Pop + YouthUnempl" = s4
  ),
  stars = TRUE,
  output = "default"
)
tinytable_hf5i35wdw194unbczm6a

FE: Domicile_named_country X X X X
FE: Year X X X X

Plot saturated effects

library(dplyr)
library(ggplot2)
library(tidyr)
library(broom)
library(stringr)
library(purrr)

fee_pre  <- 9250
fee_post <- 22200
pct_fee_change <- log(22200) - log(9250)  # about 1.40

# models
mods <- list(
  "S1: No controls" = s1,
  "S2: + GDPpc" = s2,
  "S3: + GDPpc + Pop" = s3,
  "S4: + GDPpc + Pop + YouthUnempl" = s4
)

# extract country-specific coefficients and convert to elasticity
coef_long <- imap_dfr(mods, function(mod, mod_name) {
  tidy(mod, conf.int = TRUE) %>%
    mutate(
      model = mod_name,
      term = as.character(term)
    ) %>%
    filter(str_detect(term, "treated_factor")) %>%
    mutate(
      country = term %>%
        str_remove(".*treated_factor::") %>%
        str_remove("[#:]Post.*")
    ) %>%
    filter(country != "CONTROL") %>%
    mutate(
      elasticity = estimate/ pct_fee_change,
      elas_low   = conf.low/ pct_fee_change,
      elas_high  = conf.high / pct_fee_change
    )
})

# order countries by S4 elasticity
country_order <- coef_long %>%
  filter(model == "S4: + GDPpc + Pop + YouthUnempl") %>%
  arrange(elasticity) %>%
  pull(country)

plot_df <- coef_long %>%
  mutate(
    country = factor(country, levels = country_order),
    model = factor(
      model,
      levels = c(
        "S1: No controls",
        "S2: + GDPpc",
        "S3: + GDPpc + Pop",
        "S4: + GDPpc + Pop + YouthUnempl"
      )
    )
  )

ggplot(plot_df, aes(x = elasticity, y = country, color = model, group = country)) +
  geom_line(color = "grey75", linewidth = 0.5) +
  geom_errorbarh(
    aes(xmin = elas_low, xmax = elas_high),
    height = 0.12,
    linewidth = 0.5,
    alpha = 0.85
  ) +
  geom_point(size = 2.4) +
  geom_vline(
    xintercept = 0,
    linetype = "dashed",
    color = "grey70",
    linewidth = 0.35
  ) +
  scale_color_manual(
    values = c(
      "S1: No controls" = "#4C78A8",
      "S2: + GDPpc" = "#72B7B2",
      "S3: + GDPpc + Pop" = "#F2CF5B",
      "S4: + GDPpc + Pop + YouthUnempl" = "#E45756"
    ),
    name = NULL
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Country-Specific Demand Elasticities Across Saturated DiD Specifications",
    subtitle = "Country-specific post-2021 effects rescaled by the tuition fee increase",
    x = "Implied demand elasticity",
    y = ""
  ) +
  theme_classic(base_size = 13) +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 15),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 11),
    axis.text.y = element_text(size = 10),
    legend.position = "top",
    legend.text = element_text(size = 10)
  )

Distribution graph


library(dplyr)
library(lattice)

eu_countries <- data_panel %>% filter(Domicile_named_country %in% eu_real)
eu_control   <- data_panel %>% filter(Domicile_named_country %in% eu_synth)

eu_countries$group <- "EU"
eu_control$group   <- "Control"

df_plot <- rbind(eu_countries, eu_control) %>%
  mutate(Year = as.integer(as.character(Year))) 

densityplot(~ GDPpc, group = group, data = df_plot,
            plot.points = FALSE, auto.key = list(columns = 2), lwd = 2)


densityplot(~ Applicants, group = group, data = df_plot,
            plot.points = FALSE, auto.key = list(columns = 2), lwd = 2)


densityplot(~ Accepted_applicants, group = group, data = df_plot,
            plot.points = FALSE, auto.key = list(columns = 2), lwd = 2)


densityplot(~ GDPpc + Applicants + Accepted_applicants,
            group = group, data = df_plot,
            plot.points = FALSE, auto.key = list(columns = 2), lwd = 2,
            layout = c(1, 3), as.table = TRUE,
            scales = list(relation = "free"),
            xlab = "")

Descriptives (excel)

library(dplyr)
library(openxlsx)

library(dplyr)
library(openxlsx)

desc_country <- df_plot %>%
  group_by(group, Domicile_named_country) %>%
  summarise(
    N = n(),
    
    GDP_mean = mean(GDPpc, na.rm = TRUE),
    GDP_sd   = sd(GDPpc, na.rm = TRUE),
    
    Applicants_mean = mean(Applicants, na.rm = TRUE),
    Applicants_sd   = sd(Applicants, na.rm = TRUE),
    
    Accepted_mean = mean(Accepted_applicants, na.rm = TRUE),
    Accepted_sd   = sd(Accepted_applicants, na.rm = TRUE),
    
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  arrange(group, Domicile_named_country)
desc_country_wide <- desc_country %>%
  tidyr::pivot_wider(
    names_from = group,
    values_from = c(
      GDP_mean, GDP_sd,
      Applicants_mean, Applicants_sd,
      Accepted_mean, Accepted_sd
    )
  )
write.xlsx(
  list(
    "Country-level (long)" = desc_country,
    "Country-level (wide)" = desc_country_wide
  ),
  file = "Country_Descriptives_Control_vs_Synthetic.xlsx",
  rowNames = FALSE
)

h <- hist(
  eu_control$GDP,
  breaks = "FD",
  freq = FALSE,
  right = FALSE,
  plot = TRUE 
)

plot(
  h$mids, h$density,
  type = "l",
  lwd = 2,
  xlab = "GDP per cap",
  ylab = "Density"
)

Note on how this was constructed: Assuming that there are K bin boundaries \(b_0\leq b_1 \leq \dots\leq b_K\) separated into specific intervals \(I_p=[b_p,b_{p+1}]\), the midpoint of those intervals is \(m_p=\frac{b_p+b_{p+1}}{2}\) and the bins are defined as \(\Delta_p=b_{p+1}-b_p\). \(freq_p\) are the number of observations filling into bin \(p\). The densities are given by:

\[ density_p=\frac{freq_p}{obs \cdot \Delta_p } \] where \(obs\) denotes the number of observations in the sample. By construction the histogram integrates to unity \(\left( E[x]=\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} f(x)x dx=1 \right)\), hence the discrete version of this is given by:

\[ \sum_{p=1}^K density_p\cdot \Delta_p \] where \(bin_p\) is \(b_{j+1}-b_j\) (i.e. the sum of the probabilities of observing bin p somewhere in the distribution). To show the unity summation consider:

\[ \sum_{1}^K density_p\cdot \Delta_p=\sum_{1}^K \frac{freq_p}{obs \cdot \Delta_p }\cdot \Delta_p= \frac{\sum_{1}^K freq_p}{n}=n/n=1 \]

# Total observations
n <- sum(h$counts)

# Bin widths
binwidth <- diff(h$breaks)

# Manual density calculation
density_manual <- h$counts / (n * binwidth)

# Verification table
density_check <- data.frame(
  bin_left   = h$breaks[-length(h$breaks)],
  bin_right  = h$breaks[-1],
  midpoint   = h$mids,
  count      = h$counts,
  binwidth   = binwidth,
  density_R  = h$density,
  density_manual = density_manual,
  diff = h$density - density_manual
)

density_check

R says 13 rows hence 13 bins. Lets test for some of them. The second bin starts from 10000 to 20000 and has a midpoint of 15000, consisting o of 18 obs.

delta_2=20000-10000
density_2=18/(sum(h$counts)*delta_2)

density_2

we are fine then.
